name: A167 DURHAM WESTERN BY PASS 42
units: metres below ground; typed site-investigation log transcribed verbatim. Reduced Level (m OD) column used only to cross-check.

intervals.verbatim: Firm orangeish brown and brown very sandy becoming slightly sandy silty CLAY
Brown slightly clayey becoming clayey silty fine SAND with many black coal laminations and bands
Light brown medium to coarse SAND with some black coal bands at top

Firm becoming stiff dark brown occasionally laminated slightly sandy very silty CLAY with some layers of light brown occasionally silty fine sand and some bands of soft dark brown clayey silt. Coal dust with depth
annotations: water-depth column carries an 'X' observation symbol around 12.5-13.5m

intervals.verbatim: Very stiff dark brown sandy silty CLAY with some bands and layers of light brown fine occasionally fine to medium sand and some fine to medium occasionally coarse subangular and subrounded gravel
Dense light yellowish brown fine to coarse SAND and fine to coarse subangular and subrounded GRAVEL sized fragments of weathered sandstone
annotations: completely weathered-sandstone rockhead reduced to sand+gravel; base of cable-percussion at 17.05m, rotary coring below

intervals.verbatim: Orange medium grained highly discoloured slightly and occasionally moderately weathered bedded occasionally faintly thinly laminated micaceous SANDSTONE - strong, locally moderately strong locally very strong. Occasional thin coal laminae. Moderately to highly weathered and highly fractured with many laminae and thin bands of coal from 27.30m to 23.00m. Some highly fractured bands with moderately weak to moderately strong rock fragments. Elsewhere closely and medium spaced subhorizontal to 10 degree bedding fractures are often slightly weathered and heavily orange ironstained
annotations: coal-band depth range written 'from 27.30m to 23.00m'; first figure inconsistent with this interval's extent (17.05-23.10m) and is likely a typo/misread for 17.30m -- transcribed as written, not corrected
coal laminae/thin coal bands present in the sandstone

intervals.verbatim: Grey fine grained moderately weathered thinly planar laminated slightly fissile sandy SILTSTONE - moderately weak becoming moderately weak to moderately strong. Predominantly highly fractured
Yellow fine grained highly weathered highly fractured SANDSTONE - moderately strong rock fragments with much silty clay

Dark grey fine grained slightly and slightly to moderately weathered thinly planar and ripple laminated sandy SILTSTONE - moderately strong. Very highly fractured to 25.25m; occasional thin highly fractured bands thereafter. Extremely closely spaced sandstone laminae and occasional bands up to 0.20m thick of strong pale grey sandstone. Very closely spaced subhorizontal bedding fractures. Common subvertical fractures

Pale greyish white fine and fine to medium grained slightly and slightly to moderately weathered bedded SANDSTONE - strong and strong to very strong, locally moderately strong. Orange completely weathered sandstone from 32.25m to 32.45m. Localised coal laminae within moderately to highly weathered highly fractured sandstone from 33.60m to 33.70m and 35.15m into 35.25m. Occasional coal laminae elsewhere. Closely and medium spaced subhorizontal to 10 degree weathered bedding fractures. Some major 60 degree to subvertical heavily ironstained occasionally clay filled fractures
Black highly fractured COAL - very weak. Predominantly coal dust below 37.25m
annotations: coal seam not named in the log and no pencil seam correlation present

intervals.verbatim: Grey fine grained moderately discoloured yellow moderately to highly weathered carbonaceous MUDSTONE - moderately weak. Many fossil plant fragments

notes: Wimpey Geotech Borehole Record, BOREHOLE 42, A167 Durham Western Bypass (Fig. 46); 4 sheets, scale 1:50. Cable percussion to 17.05m, then rotary core drilling to 37.75m (Dando; Hands England 36/80 tractor-mounted; 412 core barrel; air flush). Commenced 24/4/90; originator MRW; checked/approved AMcL 22/6/90. Ground level 86.35 m OD. Reduced Level (O D Level) column present and cross-checks cleanly against 86.35 at every boundary; final base at 37.75m = 48.60 m OD, matching the printed level and the index depth. 'NZ24SE/194' is handwritten at the head of every sheet -- that is the BGS reference, NOT a seam correlation. No coal seam is named in the original text and there are no pencil seam-correlation marginalia; the terminal coal (36.65-37.50m) is left unnamed per verbatim rules. Sheet-4 Remarks (verbatim substance): borehole advanced by chiselling 14.10-14.40m (1/2h), 14.50-15.00m (1h), 15.30-15.40m (h), 15.60-15.80m (1h), 15.90-16.10m (1/2h), 16.50-16.80m (2 1/2h); small amounts of water added to assist boring 5.80-10.00m may have masked the occurrence of groundwater; groundwater encountered at 26.10m; full return of circulation air throughout rotary drilling; a piezometer was installed at 13.00m and the hole backfilled 37.75-14.00m bentonite/cement grout, 14.00-13.50m bentonite, 13.50-10.00m gravel, 10.00-9.50m bentonite, 9.50-0.50m bentonite/cement grout, 0.50m-ground level concrete, with a stopcock cover concreted over the installation. Only transcription concern: interval 10's internal coal-band depth range reads 'from 27.30m to 23.00m', which is inconsistent with that interval's 17.05-23.10m extent (probable typo for 17.30m); transcribed as written and flagged, not silently fixed.
